ground_truth.title: Electrical Safety Fact Sheet | Environmental Safety, Sustainability and Risk
ground_truth.main_content:
Electrical safety refers to the control measures and practices implemented to prevent electric shock, electrocution, and other electrical-related incidents. These incidents can be caused by exposure to electrical current, electrical arcs or sparks, overheating, or contact with hot surfaces or materials.

To ensure electrical safety, it is important to follow the guidelines, laws and regulations set forth by local, state, and national authorities, as well as to implement recommended industry best practices. Important electrical safety control measures include avoiding contact with electrical sources, proper use of electrical tools and equipment, ensuring proper installation and maintenance by qualified professionals, wearing personal protective equipment, and obtaining proper education and training.

Electrical safety laws and regulations cover electrical installation standards, equipment standards, inspection and testing requirements, and safe work practices. Key regulations include OSHA, the National Electrical Code (NEC), NFPA 70E, and state and local electrical codes.

NFPA 70E, Standard for Electrical Safety in the Workplace, is a key resource for helping UMD reduce exposure to risks and avoid occupational injuries and fatalities. It was created to meet OSHA electrical safety requirements and is consistent with the National Electrical Code.

Applicable regulations include the National Electric Code (NEC), NFPA 70E, and 29 CFR 1910 regulations covering hazardous energy control, special industries, training, work practices, equipment use, and safeguards for personnel protection.

Training requirements apply to employees who face a risk of electrical shock not reduced to a safe level by installation requirements. Employees shall be trained in safety-related work practices required by the regulations.

Inspection requirements include ensuring all power tools are free of damage, maintaining three feet of clearance around electrical equipment, using grounded three-prong plugs, and deenergizing live parts before working on them unless infeasible.
with: Electrical safety refers to the control measures and practices implemented to prevent electric shock, electrocution, and other electrical-related incidents.
Avoid contact with electrical sources: Avoid contact with electrical outlets, wires, transformers, circuit breakers, and the like with and keep conductive objects away from electrical sources.
NFPA 70E, Standard for Electrical Safety in the Workplace, is a key resource for helping UMD reduce exposure to risks and avoid occupational injuries and fatalities.
All electrical wiring and equipment must comply with the National Electric Code (NEC), NFPA 70E, and 29 CFR 1910 regulations.
The training requirements apply to employees who face a risk of electrical shock that is not reduced to a safe level by the electrical installation requirements.
